title: How to cross-stitch
subtitle: The single X-shaped stitch that every counted-thread sampler is built from.
excerpt: A cross-stitch is two diagonal stitches worked over the same square of cloth to form an X. Once you can work one consistently, you can work a thousand in a row and a sampler appears.
sourceNotes:
Drawn from two public-domain references:

• Therese de Dillmont, Encyclopedia of Needlework (1886), §Embroidery on linen, the cross-stitch and its variants. Available on Project Gutenberg and Internet Archive.
• Caulfeild & Saward, The Dictionary of Needlework (1882), entry for Cross-stitch. Available on Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: An evenweave cotton ground fabric woven with visible square blocks of threads. Cross-stitch is worked one X per square. Aida count is the number of squares per inch: 14-count is the standard beginner cloth.
Six-strand embroidery floss. The cotton is separated into 2 or 3 individual strands for stitching; the leftover strands stay in the skein.
Which way the upper diagonal of each cross-stitch lies. Convention is south-west to north-east on every stitch in a piece. Consistency matters more than which way you choose.

content.text: A cross-stitch is two diagonal stitches worked over the same square of cloth so they cross in the middle to form an X. Worked side by side, the X's build up into rows; worked in rows on a counted ground, the rows build up into letters, borders, motifs, and pictures. This is the whole craft.
The cloth is an evenweave called
Aida cloth
, woven so the blocks of threads form a visible grid. One X fills one square. The thread is
stranded cotton
, separated into 2 strands for 14-count and 1 strand for 18-count. The needle is a blunt tapestry needle so it slips between the threads of the cloth without splitting them.
What you need

A small piece of 14-count Aida cloth (any size, 15 by 15 cm is plenty for practice). Two strands of stranded cotton in any colour that contrasts with the cloth. A size 24 tapestry needle. An embroidery hoop is helpful for keeping the cloth taut while you stitch but is not essential at the practice stage.
Working a single cross-stitch
Start the thread on the back of the cloth (see

attrs.label: How to start and end a thread cleanly

content.text: How to start and end a thread cleanly
). Bring the needle up through the bottom-left hole of a square.
Push the needle down through the top-right hole of the same square. This is the bottom diagonal of the X (running south-west to north-east).
Bring the needle up through the bottom-right hole of the same square.
Push the needle down through the top-left hole. This is the top diagonal of the X (running south-east to north-west), crossing the first stitch in the middle.
One cross-stitch made. Pull the thread firm so the X sits flat against the cloth, not loose and not so tight it puckers.
Working a row of cross-stitches
On a chart, every coloured square is one X in that colour. To work a row of the same colour efficiently, the standard method is two passes:
Pass one: work the bottom diagonal of every X across the row, all in the same direction (bottom-left to top-right). This gives you a row of forward slashes ////.

Pass two: work back the other way across the row, putting the top diagonal of every X (top-left to bottom-right) over the bottom diagonal you laid down on pass one. This gives you a row of complete X's.
Two-pass working is faster than completing each X individually and keeps the
top leg direction

consistent across the whole row without thinking about it. Single-stitch working (complete each X before moving on) is fine for short runs of different colours or for fiddly motifs where the row is only one or two stitches wide.
Common mistakes

attrs.heading: What goes wrong on a first cross-stitch
attrs.intro: Five things that are easy to spot once you know what you're looking for.

items.symptom: The top diagonals lie in different directions across the piece
items.cause: The two-pass method was abandoned mid-row, or single-stitch working didn't follow a consistent rule.
items.fix: Pick one direction (south-west to north-east is the conventional one) and apply it to every top diagonal. If a few stitches are wrong on a small piece, pick them out with the eye of a second needle and re-stitch. If most of the piece is inconsistent, it is the practice piece you needed; the next one will be clean.

items.cause: The thread tension is too tight on each stitch.
items.fix: Pull each stitch firm against the cloth, not tight enough to pull the cloth out of shape. Let the needle dangle every dozen stitches to release built-up twist in the thread.

items.symptom: The X's float above the cloth and feel loose
items.cause: The thread tension is too slack, or the strands are catching on the needle eye.
items.fix: Give the thread a small tug as you bring the needle through each hole so the stitch sits flat against the cloth. Check that the strands lie parallel on the needle, not twisted.

items.symptom: The cloth slips out of the hoop while stitching
items.cause: Hoop tension is too loose for the cloth, or the inner ring has been wrapped with cloth tape that has compressed.
items.fix: Tighten the hoop's screw with the cloth on the bias (pull diagonally) so the cloth holds drum-tight in all four directions. Re-tension every 20 to 30 minutes.

items.symptom: A miscounted block sits one row off where the chart shows it
items.cause: Counting drifted between chart and cloth.
items.fix: Unpick back to the nearest correct grid line, re-count against the chart's bold-rule grid (every tenth thread), and re-stitch from there. Working in ten-stitch blocks and pausing to count at each block stops the drift from accumulating.

content.text: Where this goes next

Once a row of X's feels consistent, the natural next project is a sampler border. A simple alphabet across the cloth is the traditional Victorian first piece and the one every counted-thread reference begins with. From there, motifs, monograms, and pictorial samplers all use the same single stitch in different orders.
